title: Closure (topology)
content:
In mathematics, the closure of a subset "S" of points in a topological space consists of all point s in "S" together with all limit points of "S". The closure of "S" may equivalently be defined as the union of "S" and its boundary, and also as the intersection of all closed set s containing "S". Intuitively, the closure can be thought of as all the points that are either in "S" or "near" "S". A point which is in the closure of "S" is a point of closure of "S".

The notion of closure is in many ways dual to the notion of interior. Definitions. Point of closure. For formula_1 a subset of a Euclidean space, formula_2 is a point of closure of formula_1 if every open ball centered at formula_2 contains a point of formula_1 (this point may be formula_2 itself). This definition generalizes to any subset formula_1 of a metric space formula_8 Fully expressed, for formula_9 a metric space with metric formula_10 formula_2 is a point of closure of formula_1 if for every formula_13 there exists some formula_14 such that the distance formula_15 (again, formula_16 is allowed).

Another way to express this is to say that formula_2 is a point of closure of formula_1 if the distance formula_19 This definition generalizes to topological space s by replacing "open ball" or "ball" with "neighbourhood". Let formula_1 be a subset of a topological space formula_8 Then formula_2 is a or of formula_1 if every neighbourhood of formula_2 contains a point of formula_25 Note that this definition does not depend upon whether neighbourhoods are required to be open. Limit point. The definition of a point of closure is closely related to the definition of a limit point.

The difference between the two definitions is subtle but important – namely, in the definition of limit point, every neighbourhood of the point formula_2 in question must contain a point of the set. The set of all limit points of a set formula_1 is called the Thus, every limit point is a point of closure, but not every point of closure is a limit point. A point of closure which is not a limit point is an isolated point. In other words, a point formula_2 is an isolated point of formula_1 if it is an element of formula_1 and if there is a neighbourhood of formula_2 which contains no other points of formula_1 other than formula_2 itself.

For a given set formula_1 and point formula_35 formula_2 is a point of closure of formula_1 if and only if formula_2 is an element of formula_1 or formula_2 is a limit point of formula_1 (or both). Closure of a set. The of a subset formula_1 of a topological space formula_43 denoted by formula_44 or possibly by formula_45 (if formula_46 is understood), where if both formula_9 and formula_46 are clear from context then it may also be denoted by formula_49 formula_50 or formula_51 (moreover, formula_52 is sometimes capitalized to formula_53) can be defined using any of the following equivalent definitions: The closure of a set has the following properties.

Sometimes the second or third property above is taken as the of the topological closure, which still make sense when applied to other types of closures (see below). In a first-countable space (such as a metric space), formula_54 is the set of all limits of all convergent sequence s of points in formula_25 For a general topological space, this statement remains true if one replaces "sequence" by "net" or "filter".

Note that these properties are also satisfied if "closure", "superset", "intersection", "contains/containing", "smallest" and "closed" are replaced by "interior", "subset", "union", "contained in", "largest", and "open". For more on this matter, see closure operator below. Examples. Consider a sphere in 3 dimensions. Implicitly there are two regions of interest created by this sphere; the sphere itself and its interior (which is called an open 3-ball).

It is useful to be able to distinguish between the interior of 3-ball and the surface, so we distinguish between the open 3-ball, and the closed 3-ball – the closure of the 3-ball. The closure of the open 3-ball is the open 3-ball plus the surface. In topological space: Giving formula_71 and formula_72 the standard (metric) topology: On the set of real numbers one can put other topologies rather than the standard one. These examples show that the closure of a set depends upon the topology of the underlying space. The last two examples are special cases of the following. The closure of a set also depends upon in which space we are taking the closure.

For example, if formula_9 is the set of rational numbers, with the usual relative topology induced by the Euclidean space formula_101 and if formula_102 then formula_1 is both closed and open in formula_78 because neither formula_1 nor its complement can contain formula_106, which would be the lower bound of formula_1, but cannot be in formula_1 because formula_106 is irrational. So, formula_1 has no well defined closure due to boundary elements not being in formula_78. However, if we instead define formula_9 to be the set of real numbers and define the interval in the same way then the closure of that interval is well defined and would be the set of all greater than formula_106. Closure operator.

A on a set formula_9 is a mapping of the power set of formula_69 formula_116, into itself which satisfies the Kuratowski closure axioms. Given a topological space formula_117, the topological closure induces a function formula_118 that is defined by sending a subset formula_119 to formula_120 where the notation formula_121 or formula_122 may be used instead. Conversely, if formula_123 is a closure operator on a set formula_69 then a topological space is obtained by defining the closed set s as being exactly those subsets formula_119 that satisfy formula_126 (so complements in formula_9 of these subsets form the open set s of the topology).

The closure operator formula_128 is dual to the interior operator, which is denoted by formula_129 in the sense that and also Therefore, the abstract theory of closure operators and the Kuratowski closure axioms can be readily translated into the language of interior operators by replacing sets with their complements in formula_8 In general, the closure operator does not commute with intersections. However, in a complete metric space the following result does hold: Facts about closures.
